title: BLE Power Level Companion Configuration Setting

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Typical BLE indoor range is 15-35 ft. (Outdoors 100+ ft.) for Class 1 devices. Most firmware stacks use a typical setting of +0 dBm (1 milliwatt) for the transmit power level. The Nordic nRF52840 MCU (used in the RAK 4631) supports BLE with a TX power from –20 dBm to +4 dBm. (I believe the MeshCore companion default setting is +0 dBm) Can a configuration parameter be added to the companion setup under Bluetooth Settings to provide a power output adjustment? Similar to the Transmit Power (dBm) under Radio Settings. The reason for this request is because my companion sits on a 15 ft. extension pole on the roof of a multi-story condominium (approximately 40ft up) from the patio outside, where reception is great. However, when inside the living room the BLE connection is marginal and it repeatedly reconnects and syncs disrupting whatever ongoing configuration or messaging was going on (all current typing is lost). Going outside or upstairs considerably improves the connection. I’ve changed the antenna to a 10dbi one which improved things a lot. But it still occasionally occurs and somewhat depends on how I’m holding my iPhone and where I am sitting. So, just a little more power would possibly fix this annoyance. Going outside at night or when it’s cold is not always possible. A little more power output will likely give me good access throughout the house. My alternative is to compile a custom companion version for my node. But I feel that I’m not the only one that has this issue. (edited)
messages.content_html: Typical BLE indoor range is 15-35 ft. (Outdoors 100+ ft.) for Class 1 devices. Most firmware stacks use a typical setting of +0 dBm (1 milliwatt) for the transmit power level. The Nordic nRF52840 MCU (used in the RAK 4631) supports BLE with a TX power from –20 dBm to +4 dBm. The reason for this request is because my companion sits on a 15 ft. extension pole on the roof of a multi-story condominium (approximately 40ft up) from the patio outside, where reception is great. However, when inside the living room the BLE connection is marginal and it repeatedly reconnects and syncs disrupting whatever ongoing configuration or messaging was going on (all current typing is lost). Going outside or upstairs considerably improves the connection. I’ve changed the antenna to a 10dbi one which improved things a lot. But it still occasionally occurs and somewhat depends on how I’m holding my iPhone and where I am sitting. So, just a little more power would possibly fix this annoyance. Going outside at night or when it’s cold is not always possible. A little more power output will likely give me good access throughout the house. My alternative is to compile a custom companion version for my node. But I feel that I’m not the only one that has this issue. (edited)
messages.reactions_html: 1

messages.author: NerdHerder
messages.text: Anything to help the Bluetooth range would be welcome. I also have a companion node, high-up, so this is helpful.
messages.content_html: Anything to help the Bluetooth range would be welcome. I also have a companion node, high-up, so this is helpful.

messages.author: kri5irk
messages.text: Wouldn’t this be a perfect example of where that node should be a repeater? Then you’d just have a separate companion node with you.
messages.content_html: Wouldn’t this be a perfect example of where that node should be a repeater? Then you’d just have a separate companion node with you.

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Unfortunately a repeater at this location is not useful, it is in a “hole” and would only have a single neighbor. My repeater is located 1.5 miles away further up the hill with 15 direct neighbors.
messages.content_html: Unfortunately a repeater at this location is not useful, it is in a “hole” and would only have a single neighbor. My repeater is located 1.5 miles away further up the hill with 15 direct neighbors.

Wouldn’t this be a perfect example of where that node should be a repeater? Then you’d just have a separate companion node with you.

messages.author: kri5irk
messages.text: That should be fine, it would do exactly what it needs to do.
messages.content_html: That should be fine, it would do exactly what it needs to do.

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Well it’s not really fine, that single neighbor is 25 miles away and when it’s intermittent I have no Mesh access. I’ve gone a month with not a single discovered repeater or node.
messages.content_html: Well it’s not really fine, that single neighbor is 25 miles away and when it’s intermittent I have no Mesh access. I’ve gone a month with not a single discovered repeater or node.

messages.author: kri5irk
messages.text: You said the repeater is 1.5 miles away with 15 neighbors. Then you said a single neighbor 25 miles away. Which is it?
messages.content_html: You said the repeater is 1.5 miles away with 15 neighbors. Then you said a single neighbor 25 miles away. Which is it?

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: If I move my repeater to my home as you suggested,it won’t have anything to repeat and I won’t receive anything on my companion node.
messages.content_html: If I move my repeater to my home as you suggested,it won’t have anything to repeat and I won’t receive anything on my companion node.

messages.author: kri5irk
messages.text: Leave the repeater where it is, turn the roof one into a repeater as well, and have a separate companion node that goes with you. Then the companion BLE range won’t matter, because it will be with you. The roof one will repeat to your more distant repeater which will connect you to the mesh. (edited)
messages.content_html: Leave the repeater where it is, turn the roof one into a repeater as well, and have a separate companion node that goes with you. Then the companion BLE range won’t matter, because it will be with you. The roof one will repeat to your more distant repeater which will connect you to the mesh. (edited)

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: So your solution is to buy another companion rather than change one default parameter? Having a solar companion on my roof (receiving 24/7, has a Cavity filter, Amp and high gain antenna) ensures I always receive messages. I have a Ripple T-Deck, but it is not a reliable collection point especially when it’s mobile and with its small antenna
messages.content_html: So your solution is to buy another companion rather than change one default parameter? Having a solar companion on my roof (receiving 24/7, has a Cavity filter, Amp and high gain antenna) ensures I always receive messages. I have a Ripple T-Deck, but it is not a reliable collection point especially when it’s mobile and with its small antenna

messages.author: kri5irk
messages.text: I think you’re trying to treat MeshCore like Meshtastic where you have a permanent node being used as infrastructure and as a companion at the same time. MeshCore is treated differently. You need to break down those roles into separate devices. The permanently installed node on your house really should be an infrastructure node, a repeater. I’m not going to argue with you any more about this, take from my recommendation what you will. Maybe someone else can contribute an opinion either way.
messages.content_html: I think you’re trying to treat MeshCore like Meshtastic where you have a permanent node being used as infrastructure and as a companion at the same time. MeshCore is treated differently. You need to break down those roles into separate devices. The permanently installed node on your house really should be an infrastructure node, a repeater. I’m not going to argue with you any more about this, take from my recommendation what you will. Maybe someone else can contribute an opinion either way.

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: Well, I’ve never used Meshtastic so I wouldn’t know. But thanks for your comments. I’m going to pursue making myself a custom companion with the increased power output.
messages.content_html: Well, I’ve never used Meshtastic so I wouldn’t know. But thanks for your comments. I’m going to pursue making myself a custom companion with the increased power output.

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: I retract my request. How do I remove this thread? I have just setup the ability to generate a custom version of the Companion BLE firmware for the RAK4631 and have modified the BLE TX Power setting of the nRF52. Once I get it installed I’ll verify the setting and test the BLE range to determine the optimum setting for covering my home. So, my issue has been fixed.
messages.content_html: I retract my request. How do I remove this thread? I have just setup the ability to generate a custom version of the Companion BLE firmware for the RAK4631 and have modified the BLE TX Power setting of the nRF52. Once I get it installed I’ll verify the setting and test the BLE range to determine the optimum setting for covering my home. So, my issue has been fixed.

messages.author: Submarine_Man
messages.text: I’ve tested my fix and it works: The one variable I changed was in helpers/nrf52/SerialBLEInterface Current value: BLE_TX_POWER 4 I changed it to 6, 7, and 8 (6dBm, 7dBm and 8dBm transmit power) Testing results BLE TX Power setting: 6 Using: BLE Serial nRF52 app (i) Power Level 6 Two bars solid, third bar flickering. BLE TX Power setting: 8 Using: BLE Serial nRF52 app (i) Power Level 8 Four solid bars I’m going to use 7 (three solid bars) on my Solar Companion to save on the battery, I’m going to also monitor the battery to verify that the Solar panel can keep up with the higher current draw. (edited)
messages.content_html: I’ve tested my fix and it works: The one variable I changed was in helpers/nrf52/SerialBLEInterface Current value: BLE_TX_POWER 4 I changed it to 6, 7, and 8 (6dBm, 7dBm and 8dBm transmit power) Testing results BLE TX Power setting: 6 Using: BLE Serial nRF52 app (i) Power Level 6 Two bars solid, third bar flickering. BLE TX Power setting: 8 Using: BLE Serial nRF52 app (i) Power Level 8 Four solid bars I’m going to use 7 (three solid bars) on my Solar Companion to save on the battery, I’m going to also monitor the battery to verify that the Solar panel can keep up with the higher current draw. (edited)
